1: The theme: "the righteous and the wise and all they do are in God's hands" — a statement of divine sovereignty that is the theological axis of Ecclesiastes 9. The NT deepens this: "neither death nor life... nor things to come... will be able to separate us from the love of God in Christ Jesus our Lord" (Romans 8:38–39). The "hands of God" that hold the righteous are, in the NT, the hands pierced for them — the security is personal and covenantal, not merely providential.
2: The theme: the same fate comes to everyone — righteous and wicked, clean and unclean, those who sacrifice and those who do not. This leveling force of death is not denied but transcended in the NT: Christ shares the common human fate (Hebrews 2:14) precisely in order to break it. "Since therefore the children share in flesh and blood, he himself likewise partook of the same things, that through death he might destroy the one who has the power of death" (Hebrews 2:14).
3: A shadow: "this is the evil in everything under the sun: one fate comes to all; moreover, people's hearts are full of evil." The dual problem — universal death and universal sin — is the precise diagnosis that the gospel addresses. Romans 5:12 stands behind this verse: "sin came into the world through one man and death through sin." Christ's coming is the direct response to this double evil: he who knew no sin bears the consequence of the sin-filled hearts, and in his resurrection, the one-fate-for-all becomes participation in his resurrection life (Romans 6:23).
4: The theme: "anyone among the living has hope, for a living dog is better off than a dead lion." Hope attached to biological life — the most minimal basis for hope. The NT transforms this: true hope is not merely being alive but being alive in Christ (Colossians 1:27, "Christ in you, the hope of glory"). The living dog is better than the dead lion because of the possibility of change, conversion, and encounter with Christ — the one who gives "living hope" through his resurrection (1 Peter 1:3).
5: The theme: "the living know that they will die, but the dead know nothing, and their memory is forgotten." The limit of death — consciousness ending, memory fading — is the condition Christ entered in his own death, and shattered in his resurrection. The dead who "know nothing" is not the NT's final word: "we will not all sleep, but we will all be changed" (1 Corinthians 15:51). Christ, who "died and came to life again" (Revelation 1:18), is the first who having "known nothing" in death now knows all things.
6: The theme: their love, hatred, and envy have all vanished — the dead have no share in anything under the sun. The permanent severance of the dead from earthly participation defines the gravity of death — which is why Christ's resurrection, giving him "a share" again in the world (Luke 24:41–43, eating fish with the disciples after the resurrection), is so significant. His resurrection body shares in both worlds; his people will too (1 Corinthians 15:42–44).
7: The theme: "go, eat your food with gladness and drink your wine with a cheerful heart, for God has already approved what you do." This divine approval — already given — is remarkable: Qohelet commends present enjoyment as already-sanctioned by God. The NT grounds this approval specifically in Christ: those who are in Christ are accepted in the Beloved (Ephesians 1:6); God's approval is not in doubt for the one who is in Christ. The gladness commanded here is the gladness of those who know their standing before God is secure.
8: The theme: "let your clothing always be fresh and white, and your head not lack for oil" — the imagery of festive readiness, the well-dressed participant in a celebration. The NT's eschatological dimension takes this imagery and gives it weight: the white garments of the saints (Revelation 7:9, Ecclesiastes 3:4) and the anointing of the Spirit (1 John 2:27; 2 Corinthians 1:21–22) are the permanent versions of the fresh clothing and oil that Qohelet commends for mortal days. Christ is the one who clothes his people in righteousness (Isaiah 61:10).
9: The theme: "enjoy life with the wife you love throughout all your fleeting days... for that is your portion." The commendation of marital love as one's portion under the sun is the created-good that Paul affirms as holy and undefiled when received with thanksgiving (1 Timothy 4:3–4; Hebrews 13:4). The NT adds the eschatological frame: marriage is the shadow (Ephesians 5:31–32) of the permanent union between Christ and his church — the portion under the sun pointing forward to the eternal feast.
10: Direct: "Whatever your hand finds to do, do it with all your strength, for there is no work, no planning, no knowledge, no wisdom in the grave." Paul quotes this urgency in his own resurrection-grounded work: "Therefore, my beloved brothers and sisters, be steadfast, immovable, always abounding in the work of the Lord, knowing that in the Lord your labor is not in vain" (1 Corinthians 15:58). The resurrection transforms the urgency of Ecclesiastes 9:10: work with all your strength because there is no work in the grave — and the resurrection means the grave is not the end.
11: The theme: the race is not won by the swift, nor the battle by the strong — time and chance happen to all. This observation that human excellence is not determinative resonates with the NT's consistent subversion of human advantage: "God chose what is weak in the world to shame the strong" (1 Corinthians 1:27). Christ himself does not win by conventional strength but through apparent weakness; the cross is the extreme case of "time and chance" overrunning apparent advantage — and being overrun in turn by resurrection.
12: The theme: no one knows when their hour will come — people are trapped at some evil moment when it falls suddenly upon them. Christ speaks of his own "hour" throughout John — "the hour has not yet come" — and when it arrives, it falls upon him suddenly in Gethsemane. Yet unlike those "trapped at some evil moment," he enters his hour willingly (John 10:18). He who walks into the trap that snares all becomes the one who springs it from within, setting the captives free (Hebrews 2:15).
13: The revelation of God: wisdom observed and found remarkable — the divine order that places wisdom at work in the world even when it is unrecognized. The Lord who gave wisdom to Solomon (1 Kgs 3) and whose wisdom runs through creation (Prov 8) leaves traces of himself even in Qohelet's observations. The remarkable wisdom in this example points toward the One who is wisdom personified (1 Corinthians 1:30).
14: The theme: the great siege of the small city by the powerful king — overwhelming force against a vulnerable community. The NT's corresponding image is the church in the world: "a city set on a hill" (Matthew 5:14), besieged by the powers of darkness, yet unconquerable because "the gates of hell will not prevail against it" (Matthew 16:18). The powerful king besieging the small city is the same pattern as the adversary who prowls around like a roaring lion (1 Peter 5:8).
15: A shadow: "a poor wise man was found in the city, and by his wisdom he saved it — yet no one remembered that poor man afterward." This is one of the clearest anticipatory shadows of Christ in Ecclesiastes. The structural correspondence: one poor man whose wisdom saves a community, but who is despised and forgotten. Isaiah 53:3 — "he was despised and rejected by men" — and Luke 4:24 — "a prophet is not accepted in his hometown" — take up this pattern. Christ saves the city (the people of God) through wisdom written in blood, and the one who was not remembered is now the name above every name (Philippians 2:9). The shadow here is remarkably precise.
16: Direct: "wisdom is better than strength, but the poor man's wisdom is despised and his words go unheard." Paul cites this logic in 1 Corinthians 1:25: "the foolishness of God is wiser than human wisdom, and the weakness of God is stronger than human strength." The despised wisdom of the poor man is the type; Christ crucified — "to the Jews a stumbling block and to the Gentiles foolishness" (1 Corinthians 1:23) — is the fulfillment. The words that went unheard are now proclaimed to every nation.
17: The theme: words spoken quietly by the wise carry more weight than the shouting of a ruler among fools. Christ teaches with quiet authority — "not as the scribes" (Matthew 7:29), without theatrical performance. The still, small voice (1 Kings 19:12) of divine wisdom against the loud noise of earthly power is the pattern of Christ's own ministry: not with fanfare but in the back rooms and on hillsides, speaking words that outlasted every shouting ruler of his age.
18: The theme: wisdom is better than weapons of war, but one fool can undo a great deal of good. The fragility of wisdom in a world where one fool can unravel what wisdom built is the Ecclesiastes picture of history's instability. The NT's answer is not that wisdom becomes unbeatable by skill but that Christ's wisdom — established by resurrection — cannot be undone by any fool. The one who "destroyed the wisdom of the wise" (1 Corinthians 1:19) by raising the crucified Jesus has secured a wisdom that no fool can undo again.
